filename: Djeumou et al. (2022) - Neural Networks with Physics-Informed Architectures.md
title: Neural Networks with Physics-Informed Architectures and Constraints for Dynamical Systems Modeling
summary_abstract: The paper proposes a framework for learning dynamical system models from trajectory data by embedding available prior physics knowledge both into the neural network structure (as a compositional vector field) and as hard constraints enforced via the augmented Lagrangian method. Unknown terms in the equations of motion are parametrized by neural networks, which are composed with analytically known terms such as the mass matrix or Jacobian, then integrated via a differentiable ODE solver to produce multi-step rollout predictions. Experiments on a double pendulum and a suite of high-dimensional Brax robots show accuracy improvements of up to two orders of magnitude over a purely data-driven baseline.
key_results: (1) Compositional grey-box structure outperforms black-box baseline by 100x on rollout error with the same data. (2) Augmented Lagrangian constraint enforcement drives constraint violation to near zero (4 orders of magnitude lower than unconstrained). (3) Framework scales to 143-dimensional state spaces (Fetch robot). (4) Semi-supervised constraint enforcement on unlabeled state-space points improves out-of-distribution generalization.
keywords: physics-informed neural networks,augmented Lagrangian,grey-box dynamics,compositional vector field,neural ODE,rigid-body dynamics,contact force constraints,data efficiency,robotics
potential_novelties: N1: Compositional grey-box structure (known terms + neural residuals) directly maps to the project RNEA+tau_res architecture and validates the design choice. N2: Augmented Lagrangian training algorithm (Algorithm 1 with penalty escalation + Lagrange multiplier updates) is directly implementable for torque-limit and dissipativity constraints in Stage 1. N3: Semi-supervised constraint enforcement on unlabeled points throughout state-space could extend dissipativity constraints beyond training trajectories in Stage 1.
linked_goal: Goal 3 (scaling to 7-DoF); Goal 1 (URDF-to-model pipeline validation); Goal 4 (sim-to-real via physics constraints)
notes: Experiments use ReLU activations (project requires Mish/Softplus); dynamics are learned as full ODEs with differentiable solver (project uses offline qddot for RNEA, no ODE integration at training time); validated only in simulation (Brax), no real robot; no payload conditioning.

filename: Liu et al. (2024) - Physics-Informed Neural Networks to Model and Control Robots.md
title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks to Model and Control Robots: A Theoretical and Experimental Investigation
summary_abstract: Liu et al. extend Lagrangian and Hamiltonian Neural Networks (LNNs/HNNs) to handle non-conservative (dissipative) forces and non-collocated actuator inputs — two issues that blocked prior LNN/HNN work from applying to real robots. They replace the direct-acceleration loss with a Runge-Kutta-4 rollout loss to avoid needing unmeasurable joint accelerations, and prove Lyapunov stability for both a regulation and a trajectory-tracking controller derived from the learned model. Experiments are conducted on two soft continuum robots and on the Franka Emika Panda 7-DoF manipulator (simulation in PyBullet + real hardware tracking).
key_results: (1) LNN/HNN with damping matrix (D-NN) and actuator matrix (A-NN) sub-networks beats a black-box fully-connected baseline in trajectory prediction on all tested systems. (2) On the real Franka Panda at 500 Hz, prediction error drops from 182.5 to 2.68 rad^2 over 2 s vs. black box. (3) PD-augmented computed-torque controller tracks sinusoidal and helical trajectories on real Panda hardware. (4) Training uses only 25 000 samples for the Panda versus 550 000 for the black-box baseline. (5) No payload conditioning, no URDF-based white-box term, no torque-limit constraints, and no 1 kHz closed-loop demonstration are reported.
keywords: Lagrangian neural networks,Hamiltonian neural networks,damping matrix,non-collocated actuation,Runge-Kutta rollout loss,model-based control,Lyapunov stability,Franka Panda,soft robotics,physics-informed neural networks
potential_novelties: N1: RK4 rollout loss (predicting next state instead of current acceleration) avoids needing qddot at training time — applicable to the project Stage 1 training loop as an alternative or complement to the RNEA offline qddot path. N2: Cholesky-decomposition enforcement of positive-definiteness on the mass and damping sub-networks is a proven architecture trick directly adoptable for the tau_res dissipativity constraint in Stage 1. N3: Regulation+tracking controller proofs under learned-model uncertainty (bounded delta_all perturbation argument) provide a stability template for the Stage 3 computed-torque+PD controller. N4: Data efficiency benchmark (25k vs 550k samples for Panda) establishes a concrete comparison point for Stage 1 data requirements with URDF-seeded grey-box approach.
linked_goal: N1: Goal 4 (sim-to-real; removing non-causal qddot need); N2: Goal 3 (scaling to 7-DoF; physics constraint enforcement); N3: Goal 2 (real-time control stability guarantees); N4: Goal 1 (URDF pipeline data efficiency argument)
code_available: no — data available on request from authors
notes: PRIMARY BASELINE PAPER. Critical gaps vs. project goals: (1) No URDF-to-model automation — model equations are manually derived for each robot. (2) Control loop runs at 500 Hz on real robot; no 1 kHz MPC integration demonstrated. (3) Validated on Franka Panda (7-DoF) but without payload conditioning — cannot adapt to changing end-effector loads. (4) No systematic sim-to-real transfer protocol — hardware data collected directly without simulation pre-training. (5) Black-box LNN learns full dynamics from scratch; no analytical white-box RNEA term used. (6) No torque-limit or velocity-limit constraints in the loss. (7) Implemented in JAX/dm-Haiku, not PyTorch.

title: One Model to Drift Them All: Physics-Informed Conditional Diffusion Model for Driving at the Limits
authors: Djeumou, Lew, Ding, Thompson, Suminaka, Greiff, Subosits
summary_abstract: The paper proposes a hierarchical framework for autonomous driving at the friction limits by training a conditional diffusion model to predict the parameters of a physics-informed neural SDE vehicle dynamics model from unlabelled multi-vehicle trajectory data. A single generative model conditioned on short online history windows adapts at test time to different vehicles and road conditions without any gradient updates, then feeds sampled parameters into a real-time MPC controller. Hardware experiments on a Toyota Supra and a Lexus LC500 in varying tire and wet-weather conditions show tracking performance matching per-vehicle expert models while generalising across unseen configurations.
key_results: (1) One diffusion model controls two dynamically dissimilar vehicles achieving drift tracking lateral RMSE 0.19-0.82 m and slip-angle RMSE 2.26-6.52 deg, matching per-vehicle expert baselines. (2) Hierarchical rate decoupling runs diffusion sampling at 2 Hz and MPC at 200 Hz on a laptop CPU avoiding the inference-time bottleneck. (3) With only 7 additional rain trajectories the model enables drifting on wet surfaces (1.47 m lateral RMSE). (4) Physics prior in neural SDE enables generalisation to manoeuvres absent from training data. (5) Unlabelled heterogeneous dataset (no per-trajectory vehicle or tire labels) suffices for training.
keywords: conditional diffusion model,neural SDE,physics-informed dynamics,online adaptation,model predictive control,autonomous drifting,tire model,hierarchical control,unlabelled data,sim-to-real
potential_novelties: N1: Hierarchical rate decoupling (slow generative model + fast MPC) could conceptually inspire a design where PINN payload adaptation runs at a slow outer loop while computed-torque control runs at 1 kHz inner loop -- but requires significant architectural redesign and domain transfer from vehicles to manipulators. N2: Online history-conditioned adaptation without gradient updates could inform a payload-estimation front-end for Goal 4 sim-to-real gap -- but diffusion machinery is vehicle-domain-specific.
linked_goal: N1: Goal 2 (1 kHz control hierarchy -- weak link); N2: Goal 4 (sim-to-real adaptation -- weak link requiring domain transfer)
code_available: no — video at https://tinyurl.com/diff-drift; no public code repository found
notes: Domain mismatch: paper targets wheeled vehicle drifting (single-track bicycle tire model) not robot arm dynamics; no URDF no joint torques no RNEA no augmented Lagrangian. Control rate 200 Hz (MPC) / 2 Hz (diffusion) -- below project 1 kHz target and architecturally incompatible. All novelties require major domain adaptation; relevance to RNEA+residual+augmented-Lagrangian architecture is near zero. Implemented in JAX on laptop GPU (GeForce RTX 2060). Validated only on cars not manipulators.

filename: Duong et al. (2024) Port-Hamiltonian Neural ODE Networks on Lie.md
title: Port-Hamiltonian Neural ODE Networks on Lie Groups For Robot Dynamics Learning and Control
summary_abstract: The paper designs a neural ODE network whose internal dynamics are governed by port-Hamiltonian equations defined over a matrix Lie group (e.g. SE(3)), learning separate sub-networks for kinetic energy (via Cholesky-factored inverse mass matrices L_v and L_omega), potential energy, an energy-dissipation matrix (friction/drag, also Cholesky-factored), and an actuator input matrix. The skew-symmetric interconnection structure guarantees energy conservation by construction when dissipation and control are absent, while the dissipation sub-network explicitly accounts for velocity-proportional friction. A unified IDA-PBC-derived controller achieves trajectory tracking on SE(3) for both fully- and under-actuated systems using only the learned model, validated on simulated pendulum/quadrotor and real PX4 quadrotor platforms with demonstrated payload adaptation in 100 fine-tuning steps.
key_results: (1) Structured port-Hamiltonian model achieves 125x lower training loss than black-box MLP and avg. prediction error 0.008 rad vs. 1.02 rad (black-box) for pendulum over 50-second rollout. (2) SO(3)/SE(3) geometric constraints (det(R)-1 and RR^T-I) remain near machine precision for structured model vs. orders-of-magnitude drift for black-box baseline. (3) Total energy standard deviation 0.003 for structured Hamiltonian vs. 13557 for black-box on pendulum without control or dissipation. (4) Real PX4 quadrotor position tracking error reduced 2-4x vs. nominal geometric controller. (5) Payload adaptation fine-tuned in only 100 gradient steps from pretrained model, recovering tracking accuracy. (6) Reported per-step inference ~2.5 ms on i9 CPU (~400 Hz upper bound).
keywords: port-Hamiltonian,Lie group,SE(3),neural ODE,Cholesky decomposition,energy conservation,dissipation network,IDA-PBC,trajectory tracking,quadrotor,dynamics learning,rigid-body
potential_novelties: N1-Duong: Cholesky-factored inverse mass matrix sub-network (lower-triangular L_v and L_omega, positive-definite by construction via LL^T + eps*I) guarantees physical positive-definiteness of the inertia term -- directly informs the pending N2-Liu FrictionNet Cholesky design for the tau_res dissipativity constraint. N2-Duong: Separate learned energy-dissipation sub-network D_theta(q,p) modelling velocity-proportional friction via a Cholesky-positive-semi-definite matrix -- structurally analogous to the friction residual component of tau_res in GreyBoxNet and provides a concrete architecture template. N3-Duong: Quick payload fine-tuning in 100 gradient steps from a pretrained model -- conceptual precedent for the two-step sim-pre-training then real-hardware fine-tuning protocol of Goal 4 sim-to-real gap resolution.
linked_goal: N1-Duong: Goal 3 (scaling to 7-DoF with physics constraint enforcement); N2-Duong: Goal 3 (residual friction learning with structural constraint); N3-Duong: Goal 4 (standardised sim-to-real gap resolution via fast fine-tuning)
notes: Validated on UAVs (quadrotor) and pendulum only -- NOT on fixed-base torque-controlled manipulators. Architecture requires generalized momenta p as internal ODE state (needs differentiable ODE solver at training time) -- fundamentally incompatible with the project RNEA offline-qddot path. Tanh activations throughout -- conflicts with project Mish/Softplus requirement. No joint torque limits, no payload conditioning input delta, no URDF automation. Inference ~2.5 ms per step (~400 Hz) falls short of 1 kHz target. Lie group SE(3) state representation is irrelevant for Franka joint-space (q in R^7). IDA-PBC controller targets underactuated SE(3) mobile robots, not torque-controlled serial manipulators. All three novelties are inspirational/analogous rather than directly portable.

title: Trajectory Control of Multi-Axis Robotic Arms Based on Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Nonlinear Model Predictive Control
authors: Yiqing Wang, Changqing Xia, Xi Jin, Chi Xu, Yanzhu Zhang
summary_abstract: The paper proposes DPIPC (Discrete Physics-Informed Predictive Control), an integrated method that trains a Discrete PINN on synthetic data generated via Sobol quasi-random sampling (no prior real data required) and uses the trapezoidal method to discretise robot arm dynamics. The PINN model feeds state predictions to an Extended Kalman Filter for noise-robust state estimation, which in turn supplies a Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) optimiser with a finite-horizon cost. Simulation experiments on a 3-DOF planar arm demonstrate that DPIPC reduces position RMSE to below 0.05 rad and velocity RMSE to below 0.02 rad/s compared to a prior PINN-MPC baseline.
key_results: (1) Sobol-sequence-generated data (65 536 points) without any physical measurements suffices to train the PINN in a data-scarce regime. (2) Trapezoidal predictor-corrector integration of dynamics equations used inside the PINN physics loss (replacing Euler). (3) DPIPC reduces position tracking RMSE to <0.05 rad and velocity RMSE to <0.02 rad/s vs. PINN-MPC reference [12] which exceeds both thresholds. (4) EKF state estimator coupled with NMPC prediction horizon of 10 steps improves robustness under Gaussian noise. (5) The approach is validated in simulation only; no real hardware experiments are reported. (6) PINN uses ReLU activation with 3 hidden layers x 128 neurons; control input is predicted directly (black-box control).
keywords: Discrete PINN,Sobol sequence,trapezoidal integration,NMPC,EKF,trajectory control,robotic arm,data-scarce training,physics loss,model predictive control
potential_novelties: N1: Sobol quasi-random sequence for synthetic data generation in the absence of real robot data -- could inform the Stage 1 data pipeline when Isaac Sim trajectories are unavailable, as a structured alternative to uniform random sampling (linked_goal: Goal 1 -- URDF-to-model pipeline data generation). N2: Trapezoidal predictor-corrector as the discrete physics residual inside the PINN loss -- a lightweight alternative to RK4 rollout (N1-Liu) that avoids needing qddot explicitly and is compatible with the project grey-box RNEA offline path (linked_goal: Goal 3 -- physics constraint in training loss). N3: EKF-augmented state estimator running between PINN predictions and NMPC optimiser -- could serve Goal 2 as a noise-filtering layer at the 1 kHz interface between the PINN model and the MPC solver (linked_goal: Goal 2 -- high-frequency real-time control at 1000 Hz).
linked_goal: N1: Goal 1; N2: Goal 3; N3: Goal 2
notes: Sim-only: 3-DOF planar arm, NOT 7-DoF, NOT Franka Panda. ReLU activations throughout -- directly incompatible with project Mish/Softplus-only requirement. Black-box PINN learns full dynamics from scratch (no analytical RNEA white-box term, no grey-box residual). Physics model (M, C, g) is hand-derived assuming uniform-rod links -- no URDF automation. Control output is the joint torque sequence predicted by PINN fed directly to NMPC (no computed-torque + PD layer). No payload conditioning (delta). No torque-limit or dissipativity constraints in the loss (alpha=beta=1 fixed). Trapezoidal integration step Dt=0.05s (20 Hz) -- far below the 1 kHz project target. Novelties N1 (Sobol sampling) and N2 (trapezoidal physics loss) are low-effort inspirational ideas; N3 (EKF) conflicts with the project real-time 1 kHz control loop which requires sub-millisecond compute per step. Overall relevance is low: no architectural overlap with RNEA+GreyBoxNet+AugLagrangian pipeline.

filename: Wang et al. (2025) - Symplectic Physics-Embedded Learning (SPEL).md
title: Symplectic Physics-Embedded Learning via Lie Groups Hamiltonian Formulation for Serial Manipulator Dynamics Prediction
authors: Fei Wang, Liping Chen, Jianwan Ding
summary_abstract: Wang et al. propose SPEL, a physics-embedded neural ODE framework that encodes Lie group kinematics and port-Hamiltonian dynamics into four sub-networks (M-NN, D-NN, G-NN, V-NN), enforcing sparsity in those matrices via robot-specific geometric priors and replacing constant matrix elements with trainable scalar parameters rather than output nodes. The architecture reduces parameters by over 52% versus the baseline PHNODEs model and achieves faster convergence and lower prediction error on simulated 2-link and RPR manipulators as well as a real 6-DOF arm. A KAN variant (SPEL-KAN) substitutes fixed tanh activations with learnable B-spline functions cutting parameters further but introducing a 14% overfitting gap on real hardware data.
key_results: (1) On a simulated two-link manipulator SPEL cuts PHNODEs' 277k parameters to 56k (80% reduction) with 5.8x faster training and prediction loss from 0.194 to 0.113. (2) On a real 6-DOF manipulator SPEL reduces parameters by 52.1% and training time 4.7x; total velocity prediction error drops 69.1% vs. PHNODEs. (3) SPEL-KAN further reduces parameters to 26% of PHNODEs on the real arm but shows 14.0% overfitting gap vs. 9.8% for SPEL. (4) Physics-driven sparse matrix constraints stabilise optimisation with markedly less training-curve oscillation than PHNODEs on real noisy data. (5) All dynamics learned via Neural ODE solver -- no joint accelerations required during training; validated at 20 Hz sim and 500 Hz sampling / 125 Hz control on real hardware.
keywords: Lie group,port-Hamiltonian,symplectic,physics-embedded,sparse matrix,Cholesky decomposition,neural ODE,Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks,KAN,serial manipulator,dissipation network,dynamics learning
potential_novelties: N1-Wang: Physics-driven sparsity masks on D-NN and M-NN (set non-z-axis Lie-group matrix elements to structural zero) informs the pending N2-Liu Cholesky FrictionNet design by providing a concrete recipe for reducing output dimensionality while preserving positive-semi-definiteness of the dissipation sub-network. N2-Wang: Encoding input-independent mass/inertia constants as network scalar parameters rather than output nodes -- applicable to GreyBoxNet to reduce M-NN parameter count when Pinocchio-derived nominal inertias are known from URDF. N3-Wang: SPEL-KAN substitutes fixed activations with learnable KAN B-spline activations in tau_res -- could improve nonlinear feature extraction but 14% overfitting gap on real data and conflict with Mish/Softplus requirement make this low-priority.
linked_goal: N1-Wang: Goal 3; N2-Wang: Goal 1; N3-Wang: Goal 3
code_available: no -- data available upon request from corresponding author (chenlp@hust.edu.cn)
notes: CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL INCOMPATIBILITY: SPEL is a full Neural ODE / Hamiltonian forward-simulation system learning q(t) and zeta_dot(t) via an ODE solver -- NOT a direct inverse-dynamics tau predictor. This is fundamentally incompatible with the project RNEA + GreyBoxNet (tau_pred = RNEA + tau_res) architecture. Validated on: 2-link sim, RPR sim (20 Hz), real 6-DOF (500 Hz sample / 125 Hz control) -- NOT Franka Panda, NOT 7-DoF, NOT 1 kHz. Activation tanh throughout -- conflicts with project Mish/Softplus-only requirement. No payload conditioning (no delta input). No torque-limit constraints or augmented Lagrangian in loss. No URDF automation -- DH parameters hand-coded. N1 and N2 are inspirational design patterns for the FrictionNet sub-module; N3 (KAN activations) conflicts with Mish/Softplus constraint and shows overfitting on real data.
